text: The video shows the setup for a robotic-assisted right lower lobectomy using the DaVinci XI platform. Four robotic arms are positioned in the seventh intercostal space, with the most posterior arm located approximately four centimeters from the spine. The arms are spaced about nine centimeters apart. The most anterior arm features a twelve millimeter port for a robotic stapler, while a fifteen millimeter assistant port is placed in an intercostal space just above the diaphragm.

The surgeon begins by dividing the pulmonary ligament, using robotic arm one to retract the right lower lobe forward and upward with a gauze rolled like a cigar. The inferior pulmonary vein is identified and protected. The posterior mediastinal pleura is dissected, and subcarinal lymph nodes along with mediastinal lymph node station seven are excised. Large bronchial arteries surrounding the lymph nodes are cauterized to prevent bleeding, ensuring clear visibility of the vagus nerve and esophagus.

The surgeon dissects the sump node located between the right upper lobe bronchus and the bronchus intermedius, creating a landing zone for the future completion of the posterior oblique fissure. The medial basilar pulmonary artery branch is used as a landmark for dissection before completing the anterior oblique fissure. The right middle lobe bronchus is identified and protected during the procedure.

A vessel loop is used to retract the lung, providing a clear angle for the robotic green load 30mm stapler. The surgeon dissects the pulmonary artery within the fissure, ensuring the superior segmental arteries are identified and protected. The tip-up instrument in arm one rolls the lung forward, and a vessel loop is used to create a window for the stapler. The posterior oblique fissure is then divided using the green load robotic stapler.

The surgeon identifies and protects the right middle lobe artery while dissecting the ongoing pulmonary artery for the right lower lobe circumferentially. Blunt instruments are used to create a window around the vessels, which are then encircled with a vessel loop and divided using a wide load, 30mm robotic stapler. Care is taken to ensure the staple line does not occlude blood flow into the right middle lobe artery.

The cardiac instrument in arm four is positioned between the inferior and superior pulmonary veins. The TPAP instrument in arm one rolls the lung forward, allowing the inferior pulmonary vein to be dissected circumferentially, encircled with a vessel loop, and divided using a wide load, 30mm robotic stapler. The right middle lobe bronchus is identified and protected, while the right lower lobe bronchus is divided with a green load 30mm robotic stapler. The lung is gently inflated before stapling to ensure the staple line does not occlude airflow into the right middle lobe bronchus. The specimen is then removed using a thoracoscopic bag.
The surgeon completes the dissection of mediastinal lymph nodes, focusing on the remaining sump node. A bipolar dissector is used for precise dissection around the lymph nodes.

The surgeon removes all visible subcarinal lymph nodes and uses Surgicel for hemostasis. The right lower paratracheal lymph nodes at station IV-R are dissected, with controlled retraction using arm number two to prevent tearing and bleeding during the dissection.

A finger from a sterile glove is used to remove large, unfriable leaf nodes. Xperel is injected into multiple intercostal spaces using a 25-gauge butterfly needle, marking the conclusion of the robotic esophagectomy procedure.
